title: The Boss's Boots
content:
آ آ آ آ The Shearers squint along the pens, they squint along the ï؟½shoots;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ The shearers squint along the board to catch the Bossï؟½s boots;
آ آ آ آ They have no time to straighten up, they have no time to stare,
آ آ آ آ But when the Boss is looking on, they like to be aware.

آ آ آ آ The ï؟½rouserï؟½ has no soul to save. Condemn the rouseabout!
آ آ آ آ And sling ï؟½em in, and rip ï؟½em through, and get the bell-sheep out ;
آ آ آ آ And skim it by the tips at times, or take it with the roots,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½pinkï؟½ ï؟½em nice and pretty when you see the Bossï؟½s boots.

آ آ آ آ The shearing super sprained his foot, as bosses sometimes do,
آ آ آ آ And wore, until the shed cut out, one ï؟½side-springï؟½ and one shoe;
آ آ آ آ And though he changed his pants at times, some worn-out and some neat,
آ آ آ آ No ï؟½tigerï؟½ there could possibly mistake the Bossï؟½s feet.

آ آ آ آ The Boss affected larger boots than many Western men,
آ آ آ آ And Jim the Ringer swore the shoe was half as big again;
آ آ آ آ And tigers might have heard the boss ere any harm was done,
آ آ آ آ For when he passed it was a sort of dot and carry one.

آ آ آ آ But now there comes a picker-up who sprained his ankle, too,
آ آ آ آ And limping round the shed he found the Bossï؟½s cast-off shoe.
آ آ آ آ He went to work, all legs and arms, as green-hand rousers will,
آ آ آ آ And never dreamed of Bossï؟½s boots, much less of Bogan Bill.

آ آ آ آ Ye sons of sin that tramp and shear in hot and dusty scrubs,
آ آ آ آ Just keep away from ï؟½headinï؟½ ï؟½em,ï؟½ and keep away from pubs,
آ آ آ آ And keep away from handicaps, for so your sugar scoots,
آ آ آ آ And you may own a station yet and wear the Bossï؟½s boots.

آ آ آ آ And Bogan by his mate was heard to mutter through his hair:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½The Boss has got a rat to-day: heï؟½s buckinï؟½ everywhere,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Heï؟½s traininï؟½ for a bike, I think, the way he comes anï؟½ scoots,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Heï؟½s like a bloominï؟½ cat on mud the way he shifts his boots.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Now Bogan Bill was shearing rough and chanced to cut a teat ;
آ آ آ آ He stuck his leg in front at once, and slewed the ewe a bit;
آ آ آ آ He hurried up to get her through, when, close beside his shoot,
آ آ آ آ He saw a large and ancient shoe, in mateship with a boot.

آ آ آ آ He thought that heï؟½d be fined all right, he couldnï؟½t turn the ï؟½yoe;ï؟½
آ آ آ آ The more he wished the boss away, the more he wouldnï؟½t go;
آ آ آ آ And Bogan swore amenfully, beneath his breath he swore,
آ آ آ آ And he was never known to ï؟½pinkï؟½ so prettily before.

آ آ آ آ And Bogan through his bristling scalp in his mindï؟½s eye could trace,
آ آ آ آ The cold, sarcastic smile that lurked about the Bossï؟½s face;
آ آ آ آ He cursed him with a silent curse in language known to few,
آ آ آ آ He cursed him from his boot right up, and then down to his shoe.

آ آ آ آ But while he shore so mighty clean, and while he screened the teat,
آ آ آ آ He fancied there was something wrong about the Bossï؟½s feet:
آ آ آ آ The boot grew unfamiliar, and the odd shoe seemed awry,
آ آ آ آ And slowly up the trouser went the tail of Boganï؟½s eye,

آ آ آ آ Then swiftly to the features from a plaited green-hide belt,
آ آ آ آ Youï؟½d have to ring a shed or two to feel as Bogan felt,
آ آ آ آ For ï؟½twas his green-hand picker-up (who wore a vacant look),
آ آ آ آ And Bogan saw the Boss outside consulting with his cook.

آ آ آ آ And Bogan Bill was hurt and mad to see that rouseabout
آ آ آ آ And Bogan laid his ï؟½Wolseleyï؟½ down and knocked that rouser out;
آ آ آ آ He knocked him right across the board, he tumbled through the shoot,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Iï؟½ll learn the fool,ï؟½ said Bogan Bill, ï؟½to flash the Bossï؟½s boot!ï؟½

آ آ آ آ The rouser squints along the pens, he squints along the shoots,
آ آ آ آ And gives his men the office when they miss the Bossï؟½s boots.
آ آ آ آ They have no time to straighten up, theyï؟½re too well-bred to stare,
آ آ آ آ But when the Boss is looking on they like to be aware.

آ آ آ آ The rouser has no soul to lose, itï؟½s blarst the rouseabout!
آ آ آ آ And rip ï؟½em through and yell for ï؟½tarï؟½ and get the bell-sheep out,
آ آ آ آ And take it with the scum at times or take it with the roots,,
آ آ آ آ But ï؟½pinkï؟½ ï؟½em nice and pretty when you see the Bossï؟½s boots.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
